topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom was thick with the scent of lilies and expensive perfume. I was nursing a lukewarm champagne, trying to appear nonchalant while my gaze kept snagging on the entrance. Then I saw him. Standing near the punch bowl, laughing with the groom’s father, was Alex. My breath hitched, a sudden, sharp intake that made my chest feel tight. He looked… different. More polished, perhaps, but there was a familiar curve to his smile that used to unravel me. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up, and I found myself unconsciously smoothing down my dress, a nervous tic I hadn't indulged in years. When his eyes finally swept across the room and locked onto mine, the world seemed to narrow down to just the space between us. I managed a weak smile, a little too bright, and then quickly looked away, suddenly desperate to find an escape route before I completely lost my composure.

[story 2]

I was deep in conversation with Sarah, trying to keep my focus on the tedious details of the seating chart, but my peripheral vision betrayed me. There he was. Alex. He was leaning against a marble pillar, looking effortlessly charming in a tailored suit. A strange warmth bloomed low in my stomach, a dizzying sensation that made my hands tremble slightly as I adjusted my clutch bag. I watched him interact with the bridal party, the easy way he leaned in to whisper something, and a strange ache settled behind my ribs. When our eyes met across the crowded room, the noise of the music seemed to fade into a dull hum. I felt an undeniable pull, a sudden, overwhelming urge to walk over, to say something profound, but my feet remained stubbornly rooted to the spot. I just stood there, mesmerized by the way the light caught the silver in his hair.

[story 3]

It was supposed to be a casual run-in. Mutual friend, wedding reception. I was bracing myself for awkward small talk, but when I spotted him across the dance floor, everything shifted. Alex. He was laughing, his head tilted back slightly, and the way he moved—fluid and confident—made my own movements feel clumsy and slow. I took a deliberate sip of my drink, trying to steady the frantic fluttering inside my chest. I noticed the way his eyes crinkled at the corners when he smiled, and a flush crept up my neck. I forced myself to move, to walk toward him, but the distance felt insurmountable. When we finally bumped shoulders near the buffet table, the contact was electric, a jolt that shot straight through me. His voice, when he greeted me, was deeper than I remembered, and for a fleeting second, I forgot how to breathe.
